items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: What is a tone row in serial music?
items.correct: An ordered series of all 12 chromatic pitches, used as the basis of a piece.
items.misconception: A row of notes played together as a chord.
items.random_wrong: Any sequence of notes a composer chooses.
items.why: Students miss the SPECIFIC structure. Schoenberg's 12-tone technique uses ALL 12 pitches in a fixed order before any repeats, generating melodies and harmonies from that row.
Students confuse andante (walking, moderate) with adagio (slow). Andante literally means 'going/walking' in Italian - it's a comfortable pace, not slow.
The answer examiners want is: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reich. “Glass” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: The real figure is 1952. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
